title: Corporate Card BIN Attack on B2B Software and Cloud Service Merchants
narrative: Fraudsters enumerate a corporate purchasing card BIN (typically government or enterprise fleet cards with relaxed controls) targeting high-dollar B2B merchants with instant digital delivery. Attack concentrates on SaaS subscriptions, cloud computing credits, and enterprise software licenses where MCC codes 5734, 5045, or 7372 are common. Over 48-72 hours, attackers test 200-400 sequential card numbers from the same IIN range, succeeding on 8-15% of attempts. Successful charges cluster between $850-$2,400 to remain under corporate review thresholds. Fraudsters use residential proxy networks showing consistent P_emaildomain patterns (tempmail services, 10minutemail variants) while R_emaildomain remains blank or mismatched. Transactions show abnormal card1_amt_zscore (3.5+) compared to legitimate corporate spend, and 70%+ occur during is_night=1 periods despite targeting business merchants. The bust-out phase completes rapidly before month-end reconciliation cycles trigger corporate cardholder alerts.
indicators: 200-400 transactions sharing first 8 digits of card1 within 72-hour window
TransactionAmt concentrated in $850-$2,400 range with card1_amt_zscore exceeding 3.5
P_emaildomain shows 85%+ temporary email services while R_emaildomain blank or inconsistent
75%+ of transactions during txn_hour 22-06 (is_night=1) despite business merchant category
card1_txn_count_24h between 15-35 with 8-15% approval rate pattern
ProductCD='W' (digital goods) with dist1=0 indicating no physical shipping distance
summary: Sequential corporate card numbers from the same BIN are tested rapidly (200-400 attempts in 72 hours) on B2B software merchants, with successful charges clustering at $850-$2,400 and temporary email domains used for 85% of purchases. Most transactions occur overnight despite targeting business services, and card spending shows abnormally high z-scores compared to legitimate corporate use.
reasoning: Criminals exploit weak controls on corporate purchasing cards to buy instantly-delivered digital products they can resell, staying under corporate review thresholds while racing to complete the scheme before month-end reconciliation alerts the real cardholders. The overnight timing, temporary emails, and sequential card testing reveal coordinated enumeration rather than genuine business purchasing.
indicator_explanations: Hundreds of cards from the same bank batch hit merchants within three days
Purchase amounts cluster in a narrow business-software price band well above normal
All transactions target business software and cloud service merchants exclusively
Buyers use burner email addresses while merchant contact info is missing or random
Business purchases happen overwhelmingly at night when legitimate corporate buyers are offline
Card attempts dozens of transactions daily but most get declined immediately
Digital products with no shipping suggest fraudsters avoid physical delivery verification
